question: An electric charge is subjected to two electric forces with magnitudes of 4 N and 3 N, acting at an angle of 90° to each other. Calculate the resultant force. (This is a very direct and clear translation.)

Two charges are placed equidistant from point O. The electric forces exerted by these two charges on point O are 6 N and 8 N, respectively, and are perpendicular to each other. Calculate the resultant force at O.
Two electric forces with magnitudes of 5 N and 12 N act at an angle of 60° to each other. Calculate the resultant force.
Two forces act on a charge. They are collinear and act in opposite directions, with magnitudes of 15 N and 9 N. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the resultant force.
Two charges, q1 = 8×10^-8 C and q2 = -8×10^-8 C, are placed at points A and B in the air (AB = 6 cm). Determine the force acting on a third charge q3 = 8×10^-8 C, if CA = 4 cm and CB = 10 cm.

Three point charges q1 = 4×10^-8 C, q2 = -4×10^-8 C, and q3 = 5×10^-8 C are placed in air at the three vertices A, B, C of an equilateral triangle with side a = 2 cm. Determine the magnitude of the force vector acting on q3.

Two point charges, q1 and q2, of equal magnitude and the same sign, are placed in air a distance r apart. A third point charge, q3, is placed at the midpoint of the line segment connecting q1 and q2. What is the net force acting on charge q3?

Four identical charges q1, q2, q3, q4 are placed at the four vertices of a square ABCD, with a side length of 4 cm, respectively. Find the electric force acting on a charge q0 = 2×10^-8 C placed at the center of the square.

Two charges, q1 = -1.6 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -2.4 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, respectively, which are 10 cm apart in air. Determine the electric field strength at point C due to these two charges. Given that AC = 8 cm and BC = 6 cm.

Four charges of the same magnitude q are placed at the four vertices of a square ABCD with side length a. Positive charges are located at A and C, while negative charges are located at B and D. Determine the resultant electric field strength at the intersection point of the square's diagonals.

Two point charges, q1 = 0.5 nC and q2 = –0.5 nC, are placed at two points A and B, separated by 6 cm in air. What is the magnitude of the electric field strength at point M, which is located on the perpendicular bisector of AB, at a distance ℓ = 4 cm from the midpoint of AB?

Two points A and B lie on the same electric field line generated by a positive point charge q > 0. The magnitude of the electric field strength at A is 36 V/m, and at B is 9 V/m. Determine the electric field strength at M, the midpoint of AB.

At two points A and B, separated by 8 cm in a vacuum, there are two point charges q1 = q2 = 16 × 10^-8 C. Determine the magnitude of the electric field caused by these two charges at point C, given that AC = BC = 8 cm.
Two point charges, q1 = 4 mC and q2 = −9 mC, are placed at points A and B, separated by 9 cm in a vacuum. What is the distance from point M to B, given that the net electric field at point M is zero?

Two electric charges, q1 = +1 × 10^-6 C and q2 = −1 × 10^-6 C, are placed 10 cm apart. Calculate the resultant electric field at point M, which is equidistant from both charges and 5 cm away from the line segment connecting them.

A parallel-plate air capacitor with a capacitance of C = 2pF is charged to a potential difference of U = 600 V. The capacitor is then disconnected from the power source, and its plates are moved apart so that the distance between them doubles. Calculate the new potential difference, U1.

A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of 7.0 nF is filled with a dielectric. The area of each plate is 15 cm² and the distance between the plates is 10⁻⁵ m. What is the dielectric constant of the dielectric?
An air-filled parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 49.11 pF and is charged to a potential difference of 66.3 V. Calculate the electric field energy stored in the capacitor.
An air-filled parallel plate capacitor has a plate area of 23.8 cm² and a distance between the plates of 0.94 mm. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor.
A parallel-plate air capacitor has a plate area of 12.8 cm² and a plate separation of 0.95 mm. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor.
An air-filled parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 13.06 pF and is charged to a potential difference of 86.1 V. Calculate the electric field energy stored in the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of 16.02 pF is charged to a voltage of 124.6 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of 51.55 pF is charged to a potential difference of 158.2 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of 19.75 pF is charged to a voltage of 131.9 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor with a capacitance of 32.48 pF is charged to a voltage of 162.4 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A flat capacitor has a capacitance of 61.37 pF and is charged to a voltage of 105.2 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 27.63 pF and is charged to a potential difference of 91.9 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 31.11 pF and is charged to a voltage of 76.4 V. Calculate the charge stored by the capacitor.

Two capacitors with capacitances C1 = 3 μF and C2 = 5 μF, respectively, are charged to U1 = 100 V and U2 = 250 V. After connecting their like-signed terminals (e.g., positive to positive, negative to negative), calculate the voltage across the capacitor combination.

Two capacitors, C1 = 2.5 μF and C2 = 3.5 μF, are charged to U1 = 220 V and U2 = 180 V, respectively. They are then connected with their like-polarity terminals joined (i.e., positive to positive and negative to negative). What is the voltage across them after they are connected?

Given two electric charges q1 = 9 x 10^-8 C and q2 = -16 x 10^-8 C are placed at two points A and B in air, separated by 12cm. Find the point where the electric field vector is zero, and calculate its distance from B.

Charges q1 = -12 x 10^-6 C and q2 = 2.5 x 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, respectively, which are 15 cm apart in air. Calculate the magnitude of the resultant electric field E at point C. Given that AC = 20 cm and BC = 5 cm.

Points A and B are 15 cm apart in air. A charge q1 = -12 x 10^-6 C is placed at A, and a charge q2 = 2.5 x 10^-6 C is placed at B. Find point M where the net electric field due to these two charges is zero. Calculate the distance AM.

At the three vertices of a right-angled triangle ABC (with the right angle at A), which has side lengths BC = 50 cm, AC = 40 cm, and AB = 30 cm, charges q1 = q2 = q3 = 10^-9 C are placed. Determine the magnitude of the electric field at point H, which is the foot of the altitude drawn from vertex A.
Two point charges, q1 = q2 = 5.10⁻¹⁶ C, are placed at vertices B and C of an equilateral triangle ABC with a side length of 8 cm, in air. What is the magnitude of the electric field at vertex A?

Two point charges q1 = 2 x 10⁻² μC and q2 = –2 x 10⁻² μC are placed at points A and B, separated by a distance a = 30 cm, in the air. What is the magnitude of the electric field strength at point M, which is equidistant from A and B by a distance equal to a?

Given two charges q1 = 4.10^-10 C and q2 = -4.10^-10 C, placed at points A and B respectively, in air. The distance between A and B is AB = a = 2 cm. Determine the net electric field vector at point N, such that A, B, and N form an equilateral triangle.
Two point charges q1 = -10^-6 C and q2 = 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, 40cm apart, in the air. What is the resultant electric field strength at the midpoint M of AB?

A small sphere of mass m = 25 g, carrying an electric charge q = 2.5 x 10⁻⁷ C, is suspended by an inextensible string of negligible mass. It is placed in a uniform electric field with a horizontal electric field strength of magnitude E = 10⁶ V/m. Take g = 10 m/s². What is the angle of deflection of the string from the vertical?

An infinitely long straight wire, placed in the air, is uniformly charged with a linear charge density λ = -6 x 10^-9 C / m. Calculate the electric field strength generated by this wire at point M, which is a distance r = 20 cm from the wire.

A point at a fixed distance from a charge in air has an electric field strength of 4000 V/m, directed from left to right. If a dielectric material with a dielectric constant of 2 now completely surrounds the point charge and the point under consideration, what will be the magnitude of the electric field strength at that point?

Two electric charges, q1 = -1 × 10⁻⁸ C and q2 = -6 × 10⁻⁸ C, are placed at points A and B, 14 cm apart in air. A third electric charge, q3 = -7 × 10⁻⁸ C, is placed at point C, such that the distance from C to A is 6 cm and the distance from C to B is 8 cm. Calculate the net electric force acting on q3.

Two electric charges q1 = -4 × 10^-8 C and q2 = -8 × 10^-8 C are placed at two points A and B, 16 cm apart, in the air. A charge q3 = +2 × 10^-8 C is placed at point C, given that the distance from C to A is 6 cm and from C to B is 10 cm. Calculate the net electric force acting on q3.

Two electric charges q1 = +3 × 10^-7 C and q2 = -3 × 10^-7 C are placed at two points A and B, separated by 10 cm. A charge q3 = -3 × 10^-7 C is placed at the midpoint of AB. Calculate the electric force acting on q3.
Two electric forces have magnitudes of 12 N and 4 N, acting at an angle of 30° to each other. Calculate the resultant force of these two electric forces.
Two electric forces have magnitudes of 3 N and 5 N, and act at an angle of 120° to each other. Calculate the resultant force of these two electric forces.
Two electric forces with magnitudes of 6 N and 5 N are acting at an angle of 30° to each other. Calculate their resultant force.
Two electric forces have magnitudes of 11 N and 4 N, and act at an angle of 30° to each other. Calculate their resultant force.

Two electric charges, q1 = +9 × 10^-7 C and q2 = -9 × 10^-7 C, are placed at points A and B, respectively, 8 cm apart. A third charge, q3 = +9 × 10^-7 C, is placed at the midpoint of AB. Calculate the net electric force acting on q3.

Two charges, q1 = +1 × 10^-7 C and q2 = -1 × 10^-7 C, are placed at points A and B, respectively, 12 cm apart. A test charge q = 10^-6 C is placed at point M, which lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment AB, 5 cm away from AB (or from the midpoint of AB). Calculate the net electric force acting on the test charge q.

Three identical charges, q = +5 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the three vertices of a right isosceles triangle with legs of 20 cm. Calculate the net force acting on the charge located at the right angle vertex.
Three identical charges, q = +5 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the three vertices of an isosceles right triangle with legs of 10 cm. Calculate the net force acting on the charge at the right angle.

Two point charges, q1 = +3 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -3 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, respectively, which are 12 cm apart. A test charge q = 10^-6 C is placed at point M. Point M is located on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment AB, and is 5 cm away from AB. Calculate the net electric force acting on the test charge q.

Two electric charges, q1 = +3 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -3 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, separated by 10 cm. A third charge, q3 = +3 × 10^-6 C, is placed at the midpoint of AB. Calculate the electric force acting on q3.
Three electric charges, q1 = q2 = q3 = -1 × 10^-7 C, are placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle with a side length of 10 cm. Calculate the resultant electric force acting on q3.

Three identical charges, q = +8 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the three vertices of a right isosceles triangle with leg lengths of 20 cm. Calculate the net force acting on the charge at the right-angle vertex.

Two electric charges q1 = +7 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -7 × 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, separated by 6 cm. A test charge q = 10^-8 C is placed at point M, which lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment AB, 3 cm away from AB. Calculate the net electric force acting on q.
Three electric charges q1 = q2 = q3 = +5 × 10^-6 C are placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle with a side length of 12 cm. Calculate the total electric force acting on q3.

Three identical charges, each with q = -6 × 10^-8 C, are placed at the three vertices of an isosceles right triangle with legs of 20 cm. Calculate the net force acting on the charge at the right angle.

Two charges, q1 = +8 × 10^-8 C and q2 = -8 × 10^-8 C, are placed at points A and B, 10 cm apart. A test charge q = 10^-8 C is placed at point M, which lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB, 5 cm away from AB. Calculate the resultant electric force acting on q.

Two charges, q1 = +8 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -8 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, which are 6 cm apart. A test charge q = 10^-8 C is placed at point M, which lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB and is 5 cm away from AB. Calculate the net electric force acting on q.

Two electric charges q1 = +7 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -7 × 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, separated by 10 cm. A charge q3 = +7 × 10^-6 C is placed at the midpoint of AB. Calculate the electric force acting on q3.

Two electric charges, q1 = +9 × 10^-8 C and q2 = -9 × 10^-8 C, are placed at points A and B respectively, which are 10 cm apart. A test charge q = 10^-6 C is placed at point M, which lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment AB, 3 cm away from AB. Calculate the net electric force acting on q.
Three electric charges q1 = q2 = q3 = -2 × 10^-6 C are placed at the 3 vertices of an equilateral triangle with side length 15 cm. Calculate the resultant electric force acting on q3.

Three identical charges, q = +3 × 10^-7 C, are placed at the three vertices of an isosceles right triangle with legs of 10 cm. Given that Couloumb's constant k = 9 x 10^-6. Calculate the net force acting on the charge at the right angle. Round the answer to three decimal places.

Three electric charges, q1 = q2 = q3 = 4.8 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle with a side length of 10.3 cm. Calculate the resultant electric field strength at the position of q3. Give your answer rounded up two decimal places.

Three identical charges q = 1.2 × 10^-6 C are placed at the three vertices of a right isosceles triangle with legs of length 5.2 cm. Calculate the net electric field strength at the right-angle vertex. Give your answer two decimal places.

Three identical charges, q = 1.2 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the three vertices of an isosceles right triangle with legs of length 5.2 cm. Calculate the net electric field at the right-angle vertex. Give your answer two decimal places.

Two charges, q1 = +3.5 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -3.5 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, separated by 8.1 cm. Point M is the midpoint of AB. Calculate the resultant electric field at M. Give your answer two decimal places.

Two point charges, q1 = +1.2 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -1.2 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, separated by a distance of 5.2 cm. Consider point M as the midpoint of the segment AB. Calculate the resultant electric field strength at M.Give your answer rounded one decimal places.

Three identical charges q = 1.2 × 10^-6 C are placed at the three vertices of an isosceles right triangle with equal sides of 9.7 cm. Calculate the net electric field strength at the vertex of the right angle. Give your answer rounded two decimal places.

Two charges q1 = +1.2 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -1.2 × 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, separated by 9.7 cm. Let M be the midpoint of AB. Calculate the resultant electric field strength at M. Give your answer rounded two decimal places.

Two point charges, q1 = 4.2 × 10^-6 C and q2 = 1.2 × 10^-6 C, are placed at points A and B, respectively, separated by 6.0 cm. Point M lies on the line connecting A and B, 3.3 cm from A. Calculate the net electric field strength at M. Give your answer rounded two decimal places.

Two charges, q1 = -2.4 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -2.1 × 10^-6 C, are separated by 7.5 cm. Point M lies on the line connecting the two charges but outside the segment between them, and is located 11.9 cm to the left of charge q1. Calculate the net electric field at M. Give your answer rounded three decimal places.

Two electric charges, q1 = 3.6 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -3.0 × 10^-6 C, are placed at two points equidistant from point M. Each point is 5.0 cm away from M, and the angle between the lines connecting these two points to M is 60°. Calculate the net electric field strength at M. Give your answer rounded one decimal places.

Two electric charges, q1 = 5.3 × 10^-6 C and q2 = 3.0 × 10^-6 C, are placed at two points. Both points are 3.3 cm away from a central point M. The lines connecting these points to M form an angle of 60°. Calculate the resultant electric field strength at M. Give your answer rounded two decimal places.

Two electric charges q1 = 3.6 × 10^-6 C and q2 = -3.0 × 10^-6 C are placed at points A and B, respectively, separated by 9.2 cm. Point M lies on the line connecting A and B, and is 4.4 cm from A. Calculate the resultant electric field strength at M. Give your answer rounded two decimal places.

Two electric charges, q1 = 1.48 × 10^-6 C and q2 = 2.27 × 10^-6 C, are placed at the two ends of a straight line segment 6.44 cm long. Calculate the electric field strength at the midpoint of that segment (on the connecting line). Give your answer rounded to two decimal places.
Two charges q1 = 3.15 × 10^-6 C and q2 = 3.75 × 10^-6 C are placed 8.28 cm apart. Calculate the electric field strength at a point on the perpendicular bisector, which is 4.14 cm from each charge.
An air parallel-plate capacitor has a plate area of 29.8 cm² and a plate separation of 0.88 mm. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor. Give your answer rounded to two decimal places.

A parallel-plate air capacitor has a capacitance of 26.97 pF and is charged to a voltage of 110.1 V. Calculate the electric field energy stored in the capacitor. Give your answer rounded to one decimal places.
A capacitor has a charge of 20 μC and a voltage of 5 V. Calculate the capacitance.
A capacitor with a capacitance of 3 μF is charged to a voltage of 15 V. Calculate the charge on the capacitor.
A capacitor has a charge of 30 μC and a capacitance of 6 μF. Calculate the voltage between its plates.
A capacitor has an energy of 180 μJ when U = 6 V. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor.
A capacitor has an energy of 200 μJ and a capacitance of 2 μF. Calculate the voltage across the capacitor.
A capacitor has a voltage of 10 V and a capacitance of 8 μF. If it's replaced by another capacitor with a capacitance of 4 μF, while maintaining the same voltage, what is the reduction in energy?
Two capacitors C₁ = 2 μF and C₂ = 3 μF are connected in series. The circuit is charged to a voltage U = 100 V. Calculate the electric field inside C₁ if its plate separation d₁ = 0.5 mm.

A parallel-plate capacitor is fully charged and then disconnected from its power source. Subsequently, it is placed in an environment where the permittivity (ε) increases by a factor of 3. Calculate the new energy stored in the capacitor if the initial energy was 1 μJ.
An ammeter has a measuring range of 2 A and a least count of 0.1 A. When measuring the current through a light bulb, the reading is 1.2 A. What is the absolute error?
When measuring voltage with a voltmeter, the result is 6.3 ± 0.1 V. If this is used to calculate power with a current of 0.6 ± 0.02 A, what is the absolute error of the power?
What is the total resistance of two 10Ω and 30Ω branches connected in parallel?
A parallel circuit has R₁ = 30Ω, R₂ = 60Ω. U = 12V. Calculate the equivalent resistance.
A light bulb consumes 12W of power under a voltage of 6V. Calculate the current through the bulb.
The measured voltage is 6.0 ± 0.2 V. Calculate the percentage relative error.
The length of an object is measured as 45.0 ± 0.5 cm. Calculate the percentage relative error.
The measured value is 20.3 cm, while the true value is 20.0 cm. Calculate the absolute error and the relative error.
The force measurement result: 15.0 ± 0.3 N. Calculate the percentage relative error.
A student measured the length as 100.0 ± 0.5 cm. Calculate the percentage relative error.
Three mass measurements were taken: 200.5 g, 200.3 g, and 200.2 g. Calculate the average mass and the average absolute error.
The measured value is 75.0 cm with an absolute error of 0.3 cm. Calculate the percentage relative error.
An inductor has an inductance L = 0.15 H and a current of 2.5 A. Calculate the stored magnetic field energy (mJ).
A capacitor stores energy of 1 mJ when the voltage across it is 100 V. Calculate its capacitance (μF).
An inductor has a magnetic field energy of 0.2 mJ when the current is 0.2 A. Calculate the inductance (H).
An inductor has L = 0.08 H, and a current of 1.5 A. Calculate the magnetic field energy (mJ).
A capacitor is charged with 1.25 mJ of electrical energy and the voltage across it is 25 V. Calculate the capacitance (μF).
An inductor has a current of 3 A and a magnetic field energy of 2.7 mJ. Calculate the inductance L (H).
An inductor has a current of 2 A and an inductance of 0.5 H. Calculate the magnetic energy (mJ).
An inductor has L = 0.4 H, the current through it is 3 A. Calculate the magnetic field energy (mJ).
A capacitor has a voltage of 100 V, and the energy stored in its electric field is 1 mJ. Calculate the capacitance (μF).
A capacitor has a capacitance of 60 μF and an electric field energy of 1.08 mJ. Calculate the voltage U (V).
In an ideal LC circuit, how does the total energy (electrical and magnetic) vary over time?
A capacitor has a capacitance of 10 µF and is charged to 100 V. What is the electric field energy (J)?
A parallel-plate capacitor is charged and then disconnected from the source. If the distance between the plates is tripled, how will the electric field energy change?
The magnetic field energy in an ideal LC circuit is described by the expression: W_L = W₀cos²(ωt). What is the expression for the electric field energy?
A charged capacitor has a charge of 900 µC and a capacitance of 3 µF. What is the energy (J) stored in the capacitor?
A capacitor charged to 250 V has an electric field energy of 0.2 J. Calculate the capacitance (µF) of the capacitor.
In an ideal LC circuit, if the maximum charge is 3 mC and the capacitance is 30 µF, what is the total energy of the oscillation?
A capacitor has a charge varying according to q(t) = 2 × 10⁻³ × cos(1000t) C, and its capacitance is C = 5 µF. What is the electric field energy at time t = 0.001 s?
An inductor in an LC circuit has an instantaneous current I = 1.5cos(2000t), and an inductance L = 0.4 H. What is the magnetic field energy at time t = 5 × 10⁻⁴ s?
A capacitor with a capacitance of 6 µF is charged to a voltage of 120 V. Calculate the electric field energy of the capacitor.
In an LC circuit, a capacitor has a capacitance of 25 µF, and the voltage at time t is 150cos(1000t) V. Calculate the maximum electric field energy.
A capacitor has a maximum charge of 1.5 mC and a capacitance of 6 µF. Calculate the maximum electric field energy.

A capacitor with a capacitance of 8 µF is charged and then disconnected from the power source. If the distance between the two capacitor plates increases by 4 times, how does the electric field energy change?
A coil has an instantaneous current I(t) = 2cos(1000t) and an inductance L = 0.5 H. Calculate the magnetic field energy at t = 0.002 s.
A capacitor has a capacitance of 10 µF and a voltage of 200 V. Calculate the electric field energy stored in the capacitor.
A coil has a magnetic field energy of 1.28 J and a current of 4 A. Calculate the inductance of the coil.
A solenoid is 0.5 m long, has 1000 turns, and a current of 2 A flows through it. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid.
A solenoid has 1500 turns and a length of 0.75 m. Calculate the number of turns per meter length.
The magnetic field inside a solenoid is directly proportional to which of the following quantities?
answer: Number of turns density and current intensity

question: If you double the number of turns of a solenoid, but keep its length and current the same, how does the magnetic field change?
A solenoid has 1000 turns, the area of each turn is 6 cm², and the uniform magnetic field is 0.005 T. Calculate the magnetic flux through the entire solenoid.
If the current through the solenoid decreases uniformly from 3 A to 0 A in 0.01 s, and the self-inductance of the solenoid is 0.2 H, what is the induced electromotive force?
What quantities does the self-inductance of a solenoid depend on?
answer: Number of turns, length, cross-sectional area

question: The magnetic field energy density in a solenoid is proportional to the square of which quantity?
A circuit consists of a capacitor C = 100 μF in series with a resistor R = 25 Ω. The frequency f = 50 Hz. Calculate the capacitive reactance of the capacitor.
A circuit consists of a capacitor C = 75 μF in series with a resistor R = 10 Ω, at a frequency of 60 Hz. Calculate the capacitive reactance Z_C.
A series RLC circuit has an impedance Z = 50 Ω, a resistance R = 30 Ω, and a total voltage V = 150 V. Calculate the power consumed.
A circuit has a resistance R = 18 Ω and an impedance Z = 36 Ω. If a voltage of 108 V is applied, calculate the power consumed (or dissipated).
Calculate the natural period of oscillation for a circuit with L = 0.1 H and C = 10⁻⁶ F.
Given L = 0.5 H, C = 20 µF. Calculate the natural period of oscillation.
A solenoid has 2000 turns. The current decreases from 3 A to 0 in 0.01 s. Given that the magnetic flux per turn is 2×10⁻⁶ Wb. Calculate the induced electromotive force.
The magnetic field inside the solenoid is 1.26×10⁻³ T, and its cross-sectional area is 10 cm². Calculate the magnetic field energy density.
A solenoid has n = 2500 turns/m and carries a current of 3 A. Calculate the magnetic field inside.
A solenoid has a cross-sectional area of 8 cm² and a magnetic field strength of 1.5×10⁻³ T. Calculate the magnetic energy density.
Given that the magnetic flux per turn is 6×10⁻⁶ Wb and there are 400 turns. Calculate the total magnetic flux linkage.
Given an RLC series circuit with an inductance L = 0.4 H and a capacitance C = 40 μF. Determine the resonant frequency of the circuit.
A circuit consists of an inductor L = 0.02 H connected in series with a capacitor C = 500 μF. Calculate the resonant frequency of the circuit?
Calculate the resonant frequency for a series circuit consisting of L=0.6 H and C=10 μF.
A 100 μF capacitor is connected in series with a 0.01 H inductor, what is the resonant frequency of the circuit?
Given L=0.15 H and C=30 μF in series, what is the resonance frequency of the circuit?
What is the resonant frequency of a 1 H inductor in series with a 4 μF capacitor?
What is the resonant frequency for a circuit with L=0.1 H and C=10 μF?
In an RLC resonant circuit, the voltage U = 60 V, and the resistance R = 20 Ω. What is the power consumed?
What value of inductor L is needed to resonate with C=10 μF at 50 Hz?
What inductance L is required for a circuit with C=30 μF to resonate at 120 Hz?
If a capacitor C = 2 μF resonates at 200 Hz, what is the inductance L?
An inductor has L=1.2 H. What capacitor value is needed to achieve resonance at 40 Hz?
Given L = 0.005 H, what capacitance C is required to achieve resonance at 2000 Hz?
An inductor L = 0.08 H resonates at f = 250 Hz. What is the capacitance C?

In an RLC circuit: The resistance R = 80Ω. At resonance, the current is I = 2A, and this occurs at a frequency f = 100Hz. When the frequency is f = 200Hz, the current is I = 1.6A. What is the inductive reactance (ZL)?
A voltage u = 200√2 cos 100πt (V) is applied to a series RLC circuit with R = 100 Ω, L = 1/π H, C = 10⁻⁴/(2π) F. Calculate the RMS voltage across the capacitor, $U_C$.
An RMS voltage U = 75 V is applied to a series RLC circuit with R = 50 Ω that is currently at resonance. Calculate the maximum power dissipation Pmax.
What is the total impedance Z of a series RLC circuit with R = 45 Ω at resonance?

In a series RLC circuit, at an angular frequency ω0, the inductor has an inductive reactance of 100 Ω, and the capacitor has a capacitive reactance of 25 Ω. By what factor must the angular frequency be changed relative to ω0 to achieve resonance?

For a series RLC circuit with constant components, when the angular frequency is ω0, X_L = 36 Ω and X_C = 144 Ω. By what factor must the angular frequency be multiplied (relative to ω0) for resonance to occur?

For an RLC series circuit with constant components, when the angular frequency is ω0, X_L = 54 Ω and X_C = 216 Ω. By what factor must the angular frequency be multiplied (from ω0) for resonance to occur?

Circuit AB consists of section AM (with R1 = 30 Ω in series with a capacitor C) and section MB (with R2 = 40 Ω in series with an inductor L). Given that LCω² = 1 and the voltage uAM is 90° out of phase with uMB. An RMS voltage U = 100 V is applied across AB. What is the total power consumed by circuit AB?

A circuit segment AB is composed of two series parts. The first part (AM) consists of R1 = 30 Ω in series with a capacitor C. The second part (MB) consists of R2 = 70 Ω in series with an inductor L. The circuit satisfies the condition LCω² = 1. An RMS voltage U = 100 V is applied across AB. Given that the voltage uAM is 90 degrees out of phase with the voltage uMB.
